title: Mutabal
subtitle: Smoky charred aubergine blended with tahini and lemon
excerpt: The Levantine smoked aubergine dip that is distinct from baba ghanoush: the aubergine is charred directly over a flame until the skin is blackened and the flesh is soft and smoky, then blended with tahini, garlic, and lemon. The smoke flavour comes from the char, not from smoked ingredients.
sourceNotes: Mutabal is a Levantine dip closely related to baba ghanoush, with the primary distinction being the addition of tahini. Both depend on charring aubergine directly over a flame for the distinctive smoky flavour. The dish is documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) and Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968).

content.text: The smoke comes from burning the skin. Put the aubergine directly on a gas hob ring set to high, or on an outdoor grill over direct heat, and leave it until the skin is blackened and blistered on all sides and the inside has collapsed. This takes 15 to 20 minutes for a large aubergine and requires you to turn it several times. If you are cooking on an electric hob, use a dry cast-iron skillet set over the highest heat. It will not produce the same intensity of char but comes closer than the oven.

The flesh should be very soft and have a slight grey colour from the smoke. Excess liquid is squeezed out before blending, this concentrates the flavour. The tahini is added in a higher proportion than in hummus, and the result is richer and less sharp. A garnish of pomegranate seeds is traditional in Lebanon.

Place the {{aubergine}} directly on a gas hob ring set to high, or on a very hot griddle pan or cast-iron skillet. Cook for 15 to 20 minutes, turning with tongs every 4 to 5 minutes, until the skin is blackened on all sides and the flesh feels soft when pressed. Transfer to a colander and leave to cool for 10 minutes.

Peel away and discard the charred skin. Place the flesh in a sieve and press lightly to drain off excess liquid. Transfer to a food processor with the {{tahini}}, {{lemon}} juice, and {{garlic}}. Pulse to a rough paste, do not over-process; some texture is good. Season with salt and taste. Adjust lemon.
Spread onto a plate and drizzle with {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}. Scatter pomegranate seeds and parsley over the top if using. Serve at room temperature with pitta or flatbread.

items.fix: The smoke flavour comes from burning the skin directly, the oven cannot replicate this. If you must use the oven, grill (broil) the aubergine under the highest grill setting as close to the element as possible, turning frequently, until the skin is blackened
items.cause: Aubergine not charred over direct flame

items.fix: After peeling, place the flesh in a fine sieve and press with a spoon or your hand for 2 to 3 minutes to expel liquid before blending
items.cause: Aubergine flesh not drained after peeling

items.fix: The skin should be completely black and papery, not just slightly brown. Leave it on the flame for longer, the flesh will not burn through the skin even when it looks over-charred
Peel carefully and rinse the flesh briefly under cold water if needed to remove any black bits; pat dry before blending
items.cause: Some charred skin mixed into the flesh

content.text: Mutabal sits on the Levantine mezze table next to hummus, labneh, and fattoush. Western menus often call it baba ghanoush, but the two are different. Mutabal has tahini, which makes it smoother and richer. Baba ghanoush is just aubergine, olive oil, lemon, and garlic. Both rely on the charring step for their flavour. Serve at room temperature as part of a spread. Eat it with pitta or flatbread as the utensil.
